notes.anchor: the Faberian bond has to be looked into
notes.note: The "Faberian bond" (nomen Faberianum) was a debt owed to Cicero by Faberius, one of Caesar's secretaries; Cicero meant to call it in to fund the purchase of the gardens for Tullia's shrine. Until the bond was made good he could neither pay for a property nor dare to commit to one, hence his repeated anxiety to have it "looked into."

notes.anchor: a hard contest, against a covetous person, a wealthy one, an heir
notes.note: Cicero describes the obstacle on the rival property: a fellow bidder or heir who is at once grasping, rich, and legally entitled, making the price and the negotiation forbidding. It is the recurring difficulty of the gardens hunt, where desirable plots were tied up among multiple coheirs.

notes.anchor: But more of this face to face
notes.note: Cicero breaks off the financial discussion to be settled in conversation once Atticus joins him, a frequent closing formula (coram) in the letters. It signals that the gardens purchase had reached a stage too delicate to commit fully to writing.
